original_abstract: Higher education across Africa is currently undergoing a period of profound transformation, sparked by a rapid and massive increase in student numbers. This "flash flood" of growth is unique in its scale and speed, bringing with it significant repercussions for university funding, governance, academic quality, and graduate employment. Paradoxically, while the continent has seen record-breaking expansion, the African higher education system remains the least developed in the world, leaving institutions to grapple with the dual burden of increasing access while maintaining rigor. Serving as the introduction to the inaugural issue of the International Journal of African Higher Education, this article synthesizes the current state of the sector. Drawing on the latest research and forthcoming studies, it outlines the critical themes explored in this volume—from the financing conundrum and gender inequities to the challenges of internationalization and the burgeoning demand for graduate education.
humanized_abstract: Higher education in Africa is undergoing fundamental change. Enrollments are increasing dramatically, rapidly and by a huge margin across the African continent, resulting in what can be described as a 'flash flood' of students. There is increased pressure on the universities’ finances, governance, quality of education and also graduate employment. Meanwhile, higher education in Africa remains the most underdeveloped system globally, at a time when the system is confronted with increasing access to higher education while at the same time maintaining quality and relevance. This introductory article welcomes the launch of the International Journal of African Higher Education and it provides a snapshot of the developments and transformations currently affecting higher education in Africa. It situates some of the core issues highlighted in the papers that are published in this inaugural edition of the Journal, covering topics such as the financing of higher education; gender equity; internationalisation of higher education and the drive for graduate studies in Africa.
